_comment: Campaign scenarios consumed by the Campaign engine. ABSTRACT educational content. Each scenario seeds the lab with starting vials, shows tutorial popups, and defines objectives evaluated against the live lab state and notebook. 'requires' gates a scenario behind earlier completions; 'unlocks' is recorded on completion. Fly recipe: {gene, allele, zyg: hom|het, sex: female|male, count} or {wild:true, sex, count}.

scenarios.title: 1. Foundations of inheritance
scenarios.briefing: Two normal-looking flies carry a hidden recessive 'vestigial' wing allele (vg/+). Breed them and watch a recessive trait reappear in the next generation.
tutorial: Welcome to your dry-lab. Flies live in vials; vials sit in temperature-controlled incubators.
On the dashboard, select a vial and press Breed (50) to cross the first living female and male inside it.
These parents are vg/+ carriers: each looks normal but carries one recessive vestigial-wing allele. About 1 in 4 offspring should be vestigial-winged.
Objective: breed the carriers and produce vestigial-winged offspring.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 10 vestigial-winged offspring

scenarios.title: 2. The eye-color mystery
scenarios.briefing: A white-eyed female is crossed with a wild-type male. The white allele sits on the X chromosome. Predict how eye color is inherited between sons and daughters.
tutorial: The white (w) gene is on the X chromosome. Females are XX, males XY.
A white-eyed mother (w/w) crossed to a wild-type father gives carrier daughters and white-eyed sons — the classic criss-cross pattern.
Objective: produce white-eyed offspring, then answer the inheritance question.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 10 white-eyed offspring
Answer: is the white allele dominant or recessive?
objectives.question: A heterozygous carrier daughter (w/+) has red eyes. The white allele is therefore...

scenarios.title: 3. Hidden carriers
scenarios.briefing: This stock looks completely normal, but a recessive allele may be hiding in it. Breed within the stock to expose any hidden carriers.
tutorial: A heterozygous fly (vg/+) looks wild-type but secretly carries a recessive allele — a hidden carrier.
You cannot see carriers directly. You reveal them by breeding: if recessive offspring appear, the parents were carriers.
Objective: breed the stock and expose the hidden allele by producing vestigial offspring.

objectives.desc: Expose the carriers: produce at least 5 vestigial-winged offspring

scenarios.title: 4. Lethal recessives
scenarios.briefing: These flies carry a recessive allele that is lethal when homozygous. Run a large cross and explain why the expected 3:1 ratio fails.
tutorial: Some genotypes never reach adulthood. A recessive-lethal allele kills homozygotes during development.
When you cross two carriers, ~1/4 of the offspring are homozygous — and they die — so fewer than expected survive and the adult ratio is skewed.
Objective: run a 100+ offspring cross at 25°C and observe survival drop below 85%, then answer the question.

objectives.desc: Run a 100+ offspring cross with under 85% survival
Answer: why did the expected 3:1 ratio fail?
objectives.question: Fewer offspring survived than a simple 3:1 predicts. The best explanation is...

scenarios.title: 5. Build a stable flightless line
scenarios.briefing: Establish a true-breeding vestigial (flightless) stock: a vial where every fly is vestigial-winged, so the line breeds true.
tutorial: Vestigial-winged flies are homozygous (vg/vg). If you breed two of them together, all their offspring are vestigial too — a true-breeding line.
Breed the carriers, then move a vestigial female and a vestigial male into a fresh vial and breed them.
Objective: build a vial with at least 20 living flies that are (almost) all vestigial-winged.

objectives.desc: A vial with >=20 living flies, >=85% flightless (reduced flight)

scenarios.title: 6. Epistasis: the hidden eye color
scenarios.briefing: These flies carry the recessive 'eyeless' allele. When a fly has no eyes, you cannot read its eye color at all — one gene masks another. Produce eyeless offspring and explain why eye color disappears.
tutorial: Epistasis: one gene can hide the effect of another. If eye development fails, eye pigment has nothing to colour — so the eye-color genotype becomes invisible.
Breed the eyeless carriers. About 1/4 of offspring will be eyeless, and in those flies eye color cannot be observed.
Objective: produce eyeless offspring, then answer the question.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 5 eyeless offspring
Answer: why can't you tell the eye color of an eyeless fly?
objectives.question: An eyeless fly's eye-color genotype is unreadable because...
options: There is no eye to display pigment (epistasis)

scenarios.title: 7. The temperature-sensitive mutant
scenarios.briefing: This stock looks perfectly normal at 25°C, yet it hides a temperature-sensitive allele. Find the environmental trigger: rear the flies warm and watch the phenotype appear.
tutorial: Some alleles are temperature-sensitive: the phenotype only appears within a certain temperature range.
Move the vial to the 29°C incubator (or raise an incubator's temperature) before breeding. At cool temperatures the flies look normal; warm rearing reveals the defect.
Objective: produce flightless offspring by rearing warm, then answer the question.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 5 flightless offspring (rear warm!)
Answer: what triggered the phenotype?
objectives.question: The flies were normal at 25°C but defective when reared warm. The trigger is...

scenarios.title: 8. Polygenic body size
scenarios.briefing: Body size is controlled by several genes at once. This stock carries 'large' variants at three body-size loci. Combine them through breeding to produce an unusually large fly.
tutorial: Polygenic traits depend on many genes, each contributing a small additive amount. No single gene makes a fly large.
These founders are heterozygous for 'large' variants at three loci. Breed and select to stack more large variants into one fly.
Objective: produce a fly with body size >= 1.15.

objectives.desc: Produce at least one fly with body size >= 1.15

scenarios.title: 9. Why did the wings disappear?
scenarios.briefing: The wingless gene (wg) is needed to build a wing at all. Unlike vestigial (which shrinks wings), losing wingless can erase them. Breed the carriers and produce wingless flies.
tutorial: Different genes act at different points in development. vestigial shrinks the wing; wingless is needed earlier, to form the wing field at all.
Breed the wingless carriers. Homozygous wg/wg offspring have severely reduced or absent wings (and lower viability).
Objective: produce wingless offspring, then answer the question.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 3 flies with near-absent wings
Answer: why is wingless loss more severe than vestigial?
objectives.question: wingless removes wings more completely than vestigial because it...
options: acts earlier, in forming the wing field

scenarios.title: 10. Developmental catastrophe
scenarios.briefing: Severe developmental mutations don't just change a trait — they can kill the animal during development. Run a large cross of these fragile carriers and watch survival crash.
tutorial: When a gene essential for development is disrupted in both copies, the embryo or larva may arrest and die.
Cross the carriers at scale (100+ offspring). Many homozygous offspring will not reach adulthood.
Objective: run a 100+ offspring cross and drive survival below 85%.

objectives.desc: Run a 100+ offspring cross with under 85% survival
Answer: where did the missing offspring go?
objectives.question: Fewer adults appeared than expected because the homozygous mutants...

scenarios.title: 11. Rescue cross
scenarios.briefing: A flightless vestigial line can be partly rescued by a suppressor allele that softens the wing defect. Cross in the suppressor and recover flight.
tutorial: A suppressor is a modifier allele: it does nothing on its own, but it reduces the severity of another gene's defect.
These vestigial (vg/vg) flies are flightless. The males also carry a wing-defect suppressor. Breed them and some offspring regain flight.
Objective: produce flies with recovered flight (flight ability >= 0.55).

objectives.desc: Produce at least 5 flies with recovered flight (flight >= 0.55)
Answer: how did the suppressor help?
objectives.question: The suppressor restored some flight by...
options: reducing the severity of the vestigial defect

scenarios.title: 12. Selection over generations
scenarios.briefing: Body size is polygenic. By breeding and selecting the largest flies over generations, push body size beyond what any founder shows.
tutorial: Selection means choosing which flies breed. Keep breeding the largest and the population's size genes accumulate.
Use the Population tool (or repeated breeding + selection) to combine large-variant alleles.
Objective: produce at least 3 unusually large flies (body size >= 1.2).

objectives.desc: Produce at least 3 large flies (body size >= 1.2)

scenarios.title: 13. Recover fertility
scenarios.briefing: This stock was left in a hot incubator and its fertility has dropped. Heat suppresses fertility — find better rearing conditions and recover it.
tutorial: Environment affects more than size: high rearing temperature reduces fertility.
Move the vial to a cooler incubator (25 or 18 C) before breeding, then raise a healthy, fertile generation.
Objective: produce at least 8 highly fertile flies (fertility >= 0.92).

objectives.desc: Produce at least 8 highly fertile flies (fertility >= 0.92)
Answer: what recovered fertility?
objectives.question: Fertility recovered when you...
options: reared them at a cooler temperature

scenarios.title: 14. Explain unexpected F2 ratios
scenarios.briefing: You expected a clean ratio of eye colors, but the numbers look wrong. The culprit is epistasis: eyeless flies hide their eye color entirely.
tutorial: When two genes interact, simple ratios break down. Here, the eyeless phenotype masks the eye-color genotype.
Breed the eyeless carriers and notice that eyeless offspring can't be scored for eye color at all.
Objective: produce eyeless offspring, then explain the deviation.

objectives.desc: Produce at least 3 eyeless offspring
Answer: why did the eye-color ratio look wrong?
objectives.question: The expected eye-color ratio deviated because...
options: eyeless flies can't show eye color (epistasis)

scenarios.title: 15. Final thesis project
scenarios.briefing: Your capstone: establish a stable, true-breeding flightless line and defend your understanding of how it breeds true.
tutorial: A true-breeding line is homozygous for the trait, so every offspring shows it.
Build a vial of at least 20 living flies that are almost all flightless, then answer the final question.
Congratulations on reaching the final project!

objectives.desc: A vial with >=20 living flies, >=85% flightless
Answer: why does a vestigial line breed true?
objectives.question: A vestigial (vg/vg) line breeds true because...
options: both parents are homozygous, so all offspring are vg/vg
